articles.title: Reliability and clinical utility of paediatric ankle fracture classifications : a systematic review.
articles.authors: Teo LS, Xiao L, Yeo BKK, Rayapati R, Lim AKS, Hoipo Hui J, et al.
articles.outcomes: Inter- and intraobserver reliability of classification systems; clinical utility in guiding treatment and predicting outcomes including premature physeal closure and angular deformity
articles.take_home: The Dias-Tachdjian classification was most reliable for paediatric ankle fractures, whilst the Salter-Harris classification was most commonly used and best at predicting physeal closure. Using both anatomical and mechanistic classification systems together may help improve treatment planning and predictions of outcomes.

articles.title: Long-term Outcomes After First Metatarsophalangeal Joint Arthrodesis: With Focus on Degenerative Changes on Adjacent Joints.
articles.authors: Recheis S, Mattausch D, Kuehn N, Kindermann H, Ortmaier R, Bischofreiter M, et al.
articles.population: Patients who underwent isolated first metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis
articles.sample_size: 77 patients (45 female, 32 male) followed up at mean 106.9 months
articles.outcomes: Radiographic osteoarthritis progression in adjacent joints using Kellgren-Lawrence classification; clinical outcomes measured by AOFAS HMI, FAOS, and FFI scores; patient satisfaction; complications
articles.take_home: First metatarsophalangeal joint fusion provides excellent long-term satisfaction and function, with over 90% of patients reporting good or excellent outcomes. Whilst arthritis developed in nearby joints over time, particularly in the big toe joint itself, these changes did not affect how well patients actually functioned or felt.

articles.title: Multiplanar Stability After Syndesmotic Screw Fixation vs Deltoid Ligament Repair in a Cadaveric Weber B/SER4b Ankle Fracture Model With Fibular Plate Fixation.
articles.authors: Haanaes EKO, Dalen AF, Gregersen MG, Skrede A, Molund M
articles.population: Fresh frozen human cadaveric ankle specimens
articles.outcomes: Talar shift (lateral translation), valgus tilt, and internal and external rotation under constant axial load measured on fluoroscopic images
articles.take_home: Both syndesmotic screw fixation and deltoid ligament repair restore side-to-side stability of the ankle when combined with fibular plate fixation in this fracture model. However, deltoid ligament repair was superior at restoring rotational and tilting stability, suggesting it may provide more comprehensive stabilisation than syndesmotic screw fixation alone.

articles.title: Patient acceptable symptom state for European Foot & Ankle Society Score After Hallux Valgus Surgery.
articles.authors: Lee HJ, Yew KSA, Yeo W, Mohamed Buhary KS, Toh RX, Li Z, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using prospectively collected data
articles.sample_size: 361 patients (324 female, 37 male) followed for 2 years
articles.outcomes: Patient acceptable symptom state (PASS) thresholds for European Foot & Ankle Society (EFAS) Score: 20.5 at 6 months and 23.5 at 2 years. AOFAS PASS thresholds: 84.5 at 6 months and 82.5 at 2 years.
articles.take_home: Researchers have identified the score that represents a satisfactory outcome after bunion surgery for the EFAS measure, which can help clinicians and patients understand whether surgery has achieved an acceptable result. The study found these target scores at both 6 months and 2 years after various types of bunion surgery.

articles.title: EFAS Score - validation of the Norwegien version by the Score Committee of the European Foot and Ankle Society (EFAS).
articles.authors: Richter M, Agren PH, Besse JL, Coester M, Kofoed H, Maffulli N, et al.
articles.population: Patients with a wide variety of foot and ankle pathologies
articles.outcomes: Internal consistency (Cronbach's Alpha 0.86), Standard Error of Measurement (0.31), responsiveness (effect size 1.05), improvement in EFAS score at follow-up
articles.take_home: The Norwegian version of the EFAS Score has been successfully validated as a reliable tool for measuring foot and ankle outcomes, with 77% of patients showing improvement after surgery. The score works as well in Norwegian as it does in other languages.
